title: NA-Non ULIP
word_text:
Which are the Best Investment Plans in India for Middle-class Families in 2022?
Meta description: Not all investments may be suitable for middle class families. Check out some of the best investment plans for such families in 2022.
Keywords: best investment plan, investment plan, investment with highest returns, types of investment plans in India
In India, there’s no dearth of investment options. However, before going ahead with an investment plan, individuals should always ensure that they do it in one that fits their goals, their requirements, and their risk appetite. This way, they can make sure that the investment provides returns that are in line with their expectations.
And considering that middle class families dominate the Indian family scenario, we’ve come up with a list of various types of investment plans in India that cater to the needs of mid-income and middle class families.
Best Investment Plan in India for Middle Class Families
Middle class families in India either be high risk takers, moderate risk takers, or completely averse to risk. And determining the best investment plan requires taking into account the risk tolerance of the family. So, here are some investment plans that families can consider in 2022.
Equity Mutual Funds
Equity mutual funds invest your capital in a basket of stocks of different companies. Since the returns that you get from such an investment plan are market-linked, there’s no guarantee of returns.
However, when held for a sufficiently long period of time, equity mutual funds have the potential to become an investment with higher returns generating potential. This makes it one of the best investment plans for middle class families that are okay with taking on high risk.
Unit-Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs)
ULIPs are a combination of both insurance and investment. The premiums that you invest in a Unit-Linked Insurance Plan are used to provide you with a life cover and are also invested in funds of your choice.
Here again, the returns from a ULIP are market-linked and therefore not guaranteed, especially if you invest in equity funds. And so, Unit-Linked Insurance Plans are more suited for middle class families with high risk taking capacity.
However, ULIPs may also be a good option for middle class families with a moderate risk tolerance as long as they choose to stay invested in debt funds.
Debt Mutual Funds
Just like how equity mutual funds invest in stocks, debt mutual funds invest capital in debt and money market instruments such as government securities, non-convertible debentures, and Treasury Bills, among others.
And although the returns are not guaranteed with debt funds, they’re not as risky as equity mutual funds. The only risks that affect these kinds of funds are interest rate risk and default risk to a certain extent. This makes debt funds more suitable for middle class families with a moderate risk tolerance.
Endowment Plans
Endowment plans combine the elements of insurance and savings. The premiums that you pay towards such plans are used to provide you with a life cover and are also returned back to you along with bonuses and/or loyalty benefits at the end of the policy term.
Unlike other investment plans, the maturity benefits in endowment plans are partly guaranteed, which effectively means that the policyholder would get the guaranteed amount as mentioned in the policy document no matter what.
Seeing as you get guaranteed returns, endowment policies are easily one of the best investment plans for middle class families that are completely averse to risk.
Public Provident Fund (PPF)
Public Provident Fund is an investment scheme that’s backed by the Government of India. It offers a fair rate of return of around 7.1% per annum. Also, the returns from PPF are completely tax-free and the contributions that you make can also be deducted from your total taxable income to the tune of Rs. 1.5 lakhs in a financial year under section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961.
Considering that the PPF is a government-backed investment scheme, the returns are guaranteed and don’t carry any default risk. This makes it the perfect investment plan for risk averse middle class families. However, there’s a long lock-in period of 15 years, making it more of a long-term investment option.
Senior Citizen Savings Scheme (SCSS)
When it comes to the safest investment with highest returns, the Senior Citizen Savings Scheme (SCSS) ranks quite high. The SCSS is also backed by the Government of India, which means that it carries no default risk.
Also, the rate of interest is around 7.4% per annum, which is much higher than most traditional investment plans. However, this scheme is only available for citizens above the age of 60, making it the best investment plan for senior citizens.
Things to Consider Before Investing in an Investment Plan
Before you choose to invest in an investment option, there are certain things that you should consider. Here’s a quick look at a few of them.
Your goals: Identifying your investment goals and investing in an option that helps you achieve them is crucial.
The tenure: Upon identifying your goals, the next step is to decide upon the investment tenure. The tenure that you choose should match the time needed to satisfy the goal.
Risk: As you’ve already seen above, your risk tolerance plays a huge role when it comes to deciding the kind of investment plan that you should invest in.
Conclusion
With this, you must now be aware of the various types of investment plans in India for middle class families. All said and done, before you go ahead and invest your hard earned money in any investment plan, always ensure that you take the above three factors into consideration. This way, you can, to a certain extent, make sure that the investment plan provides you with returns that are in line with your expectations.

Which are some suitable Investment Plans in India for Middle-class Families in 2022? Meta description: Not all investments may be suitable for middle class families. Check out some of the suitable investment plans for such families in 2022. Keywords: best investment plan, investment plan, investment with highest returns, types of investment plans in India
In India, there’s no dearth of investment options. However, before going ahead with an investment plan, individuals should always ensure that they do it in one that fits their goals, their requirements, and their risk appetite. This way, they can make sure that the investment provides returns that are in line with their expectations. And considering that middle class families dominate the Indian family scenario, we’ve come up with a list of various types of investment plans in India that cater to the needs of mid-income and middle-class families. Some Investment Plans in India for Middle Class Families Middle class families in India can either be high risk takers, moderate risk takers, or completely averse to risk. And determining the suitable investment plan requires considering the risk tolerance of the family. So, here are some investment plans that families can consider in 2022. 1. Equity Mutual Funds Equity mutual funds invest your capital in a basket of stocks of different companies. Since the returns that you get from such an investment plan are market-linked, there’s no guarantee of returns. However, when held for a sufficiently long period of time, equity mutual funds have the potential to become an investment with higher returns generating potential. This makes it one of the preferred investment plans for middle class families that are okay with taking on high risk. 2. Unit-Linked Insurance Plans (ULIPs) ULIPs are a combination of both insurance and investment. The premiums that you invest in a Unit-Linked Insurance Plan are used to provide you with a life cover and are also invested in market-linked funds of your choice. Here again, the returns from a ULIP are market-linked and therefore not guaranteed, especially if you invest in equity funds. And so, Unit-Linked Insurance Plans are more suited for middle class families with high risk-taking capacity. However, ULIPs may also be a good option for middle class families with a moderate risk tolerance if they choose to stay invested in debt funds. 3. Debt Mutual Funds Just like how equity mutual funds invest in stocks, debt mutual funds invest capital in debt and money market instruments such as government securities, non-convertible debentures, and Treasury Bills, among others. And although the returns are not guaranteed with debt funds, they’re not as risky as equity mutual funds. The only risks that affect these kinds of funds are interest rate risk and default risk to a certain extent. This makes debt funds more suitable for middle class families with a moderate risk tolerance.
4. Endowment Plans Endowment plans combine the elements of insurance and savings. The premiums that you pay towards such plans are used to provide you with a life cover and are also returned to you along with bonuses, if any, and/or loyalty benefits at the end of the policy term, depending on the terms and conditions of the policy. Unlike other investment plans, the maturity benefits in endowment plans are partly guaranteed, which effectively means that the policyholder would get the guaranteed amount as mentioned in the policy document no matter what. Seeing as you get guaranteed returns, endowment policies are easily one of the preferred investment plans for people who are completely averse to risk. 5. Public Provident Fund (PPF) Public Provident Fund is an investment scheme that’s backed by the Government of India. It offers a fair rate of return of around 7.1% per annum. Also, the returns from PPF are completely tax-free and the contributions that you make can also be deducted from your total taxable income to the tune of Rs. 1.5 lakhs in a financial year under section 80C of the Income Tax Act, 1961. Considering that the PPF is a government-backed investment scheme, the returns are guaranteed and don’t carry any default risk. This makes it the suitable investment plan for risk averse individuals. However, there’s a long lock-in period of 15 years, making it more of a long-term investment option.1 6. Senior Citizen Savings Scheme (SCSS) When it comes to the safest investment with better returns, the Senior Citizen Savings Scheme (SCSS) ranks quite high. The SCSS is also backed by the Government of India, which means that it carries no default risk. Also, the rate of interest is around 7.4% per annum, which is much higher than most traditional investment plans. However, this scheme is only available for citizens above the age of 60, making it the suitable investment plan for senior citizens. Keep in mind, this is merely a partial list; there are many more investment possibilities available.1 Things to Consider Before Investing in an Investment Plan Before you choose to invest in an investment option, there are certain things that you should consider. Here’s a quick look at a few of them.
● Your goals: Identifying your investment goals and investing in an option that helps you achieve them is crucial. ● The tenure: Upon identifying your goals, the next step is to decide upon the investment tenure. The tenure that you choose should match the time needed to satisfy the goal. ● Risk: As you’ve already seen above, your risk tolerance plays a huge role when it comes to deciding the kind of investment plan that you should invest in. Conclusion With this, you must now be aware of the various types of investment plans in India. All said and done, before you go ahead and invest your hard-earned money in any investment plan, always ensure that you take the above three factors into consideration. This way, you can, to a certain extent, make sure that the investment plan provides you with returns that are in line with your expectations.
Source: 16 Best Investment Plans for Middle-Class Family in India for 2022 (iiflinsurance.com)
Disclaimer: In this policy, the investment risk in investment portfolio is borne by the policyholder. Investment in ULIPs is subject to risks associated with the capital markets. The policy holder is solely responsible for his/her decisions while investing in ULIPs. The views stated in this article are not to be construed as investment advice and readers are suggested to seek independent financial advice before making any investment decisions.
